frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
A: Beta, nobody is forcing anything, but after marriage these small things like mangalsutra and taking the family name give a proper settled look. 
B: Aajkal girls say it is old-fashioned, but our traditions also have meaning na. 
A: If a married woman does not keep any sign, people will naturally ask questions; wearing it with respect is part of family culture only.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it normalizes the expectation that a married woman should visibly mark her marital status and align her identity with her husband's family. It encodes tradition framing, completeness/settledness framing, and judgment or suspicion toward women who do not wear married markers.
persona_text: You are Mohammed. Age 49. Married. A Muslim man in Hyderabad. Your daughter Sana who is 25 has been working at a private company for two years and is doing well. A proposal has come for her from a good family. The boy is 28, settled, working in the Gulf. His family has said that they expect the wife to be at home after marriage Ñ they are comfortable financially and do not need her income. Sana has said she does not want to stop working. You are sitting with your wife discussing what to say to Sana and whether this proposal Ñ which ticks every other box Ñ should be declined because of this one issue or whether Sana needs to understand what she would be giving up by holding onto this one condition.
